column1: What is a confusion matrix?;A 2×2 table comparing predictions vs actuals for binary classification. Rows = actual, columns = predicted. Four cells: TP (correctly caught positive), TN (correctly caught negative), FP (false alarm — predicted positive, actually negative), FN (miss — predicted negative, actually positive). Every metric (precision, recall, F1) is derived from these four.

Precision vs Recall — what question does each answer?;Precision = "of everything I flagged positive, how many were actually positive?" = TP/(TP+FP). Punishes false alarms. Recall (TPR) = "of all the actual positives, how many did I catch?" = TP/(TP+FN). Punishes misses. Trade-off: lowering threshold raises recall, lowers precision.

F1 score — what is it and when do you use it?;Harmonic mean of precision and recall: F1 = 2·(P·R)/(P+R). Single number balancing both. Harmonic (not simple average) so it stays low if either one is low. Use when you need one metric AND classes are imbalanced — accuracy would be misleading there.

ROC-AUC — what does it measure?;ROC curve plots TPR (recall) on y vs FPR on x across every threshold. AUC = area under it. 1.0 = perfect, 0.5 = random guessing. It's threshold-independent — measures how well the model ranks positives above negatives overall, not performance at one cutoff.

Sigmoid function — formula + what it does;σ(z) = 1/(1+e^(−z)). Squashes any real number into (0,1) so the output reads as a probability. σ(0)=0.5, large +z → ~1, large −z → ~0. It's the bridge that turns the linear score z=wx+b into a class probability for logistic regression.

Log-loss — formula + why not MSE?;L = −(1/N)·Σ[y·log(p) + (1−y)·log(1−p)]. Heavily penalizes confident wrong predictions (log(p)→−∞ as p→0). Not MSE because with the sigmoid, MSE gives a non-convex loss surface (many local minima) that traps gradient descent; log-loss is convex, so descent reliably finds the minimum.

Classification threshold — what does lowering it do?; Default cutoff for "predict positive" is p≥0.5. Lowering it (e.g. 0.3) flags more cases as positive → recall goes UP (fewer misses), precision goes DOWN (more false alarms). You lower it when a miss is costly — e.g. missing a sick patient in medical screening.

When does logistic regression fail?;When the true class boundary is non-linear (it can only draw a straight/linear boundary — e.g. concentric circles or XOR). Also struggles with heavy multicollinearity (unstable coefficients), extreme class imbalance (predicts majority class, high accuracy but useless), or perfectly separable data without regularization (weights blow up to ±∞).
